text: The patient is positioned supine with a reversed Trendelenburg and slight left tilt. The robotic cart is docked at the head. A 12mm camera port is placed in the left pararectal line at the umbilicus level, with alternative placement options for better visualization. Additional ports are positioned as shown. The stomach is lifted cranially, and the gastrocolic ligament is dissected up to the short gastric vessels, which are preserved. The right gastroepiploic artery is dissected and divided one centimeter from its origin, keeping retropancreatic lymph nodes attached to the specimen. The portal vein is tunneled, exploring the confluence with the superior mesenteric vein at the pancreas neck using harmonic shears and blunt dissection with a suction cannula.

The surgeon transects the stomach proximal to the antrum using endo-GIA staplers. The gastroduodenal artery stump is dissected, clipped, and divided, with care taken to identify and suture small tributaries of the portal vein before proceeding.

The surgeon performs a wide takedown of the hepatic colonic flexure, extending medially to the origin of the right colonic vessels and sometimes down to the cecum. This is followed by an extensive Cattell-Braasch maneuver, detaching the head of the pancreas from the retroperitoneal space for safer uncinate process dissection. The left side of the aorta serves as the lateral edge landmark, with the left renal vein and superior mesenteric artery marking the completion of the maneuver. Enlarged lymph nodes in the inter-aortocaval space are harvested during lymphadenectomy. The duodenal-jejunal flexure is divided, and the right-sided D-rotation of the duodenum is performed to mobilize the fourth part of the duodenum. The first jejunal loop is divided with endo-GIA staplers, and an enterocrate cholecystectomy is performed down to the cystic duct.

The surgeon divides the neck of the pancreas anteriorly to the portal vein using harmonic shears, ensuring optimal exposure of the inferior and superior margins. The pancreatic duct is identified and cannulated with an endoluminal stent, with care taken to reopen and stent the duct if sealed by thermal spread.

The surgeon employs a right-to-left approach for the uncinate process dissection, moving from below upwards. Hemostatic clips are used to control bleeding from Henle's trunk, followed by division with a harmonic scalpel. The superior mesenteric vein is retracted to the left using a suction cannula to enhance visualization of the surgical field posterior to the vein.

The surgeon carefully dissects the inferior pancreaticoduodenal artery circumferentially using a harmonic scalpel and secures it with hemostatic clips to prevent retraction and control bleeding. The dissection progresses through three layers: the portal layer with tributaries clipped and cut, the arterial layer with the inferior and superior pancreaticoduodenal arteries, and the lymphatic layer managed with the harmonic scalpel. The common bile duct is divided cranially to the cystic duct origin using cold scissors or diathermy, depending on its thickness.

The surgeon completes the dissection and places the specimen in an endobag. Reconstruction begins with a jejunal loop passed through the mesocolic route to the upper quadrants for digestive reconstruction. The hepaticojejunostomy is performed in an end-to-side fashion using running sutures for the posterior row and interrupted stitches for the anterior row, starting from the right side of the duct.

The surgeon performs a pancreaticojejunostomy using a robotic approach, providing a magnified view similar to a surgical microscope. Depending on the pancreatic parenchyma texture and duct diameter, either a gland-to-mucosa or duct-to-mucosa anastomosis is chosen. For a fragile pancreas or small duct, the gland-to-mucosa technique is preferred, while a firm pancreas or larger duct favors duct-to-mucosa. The posterior capsule of the pancreatic stump is anchored to the jejunal serosa with polypropylene sutures, and a small opening in the jejunal mucosa is created for anastomosis with PDS interrupted stitches. A stent is placed inside the duct, and additional sutures secure the anterior aspect of the pancreatic capsule to the jejunum.

The surgeon performs a gastrojejunostomy on the same jejunal loop as the biliary anastomosis, positioned forty centimeters distally. An omega loop gastrojejunostomy is created using inter-GIA staplers, and the enterotomy site is closed with continuous Vicryl or PDS-III-Zero sutures. A nasojejunal tube is passed into the jejunal limb. The specimen is extracted through a small upper midline incision, and three drains are placed: one near the pancreatic anastomosis, one near the biliary anastomosis, and one in the pelvis.
